title: Samsung Unpacked lands July 22, GPT-5.6 goes default & the AI-picking playbook
intro: Today's five: Samsung's foldable-heavy Unpacked set for July 22, GPT-5.6 quietly becoming ChatGPT's default, a Gmail anti-spam trick, an honest ChatGPT-vs-Gemini-vs-Claude split, and why megapixels don't make the camera.

posts.hook: Samsung Unpacked is July 22 — and it's all about foldables
posts.body: Samsung Galaxy Unpacked is officially set for July 22, and it's shaping up to be a foldable-heavy show. Expect the Galaxy Z Fold 8 alongside a larger Z Fold 8 Ultra, a lighter Z Flip 8 (around 180g), the Watch 9 series, and possibly a first look at Samsung's Galaxy Glasses. The creases keep shrinking — but so does your wallet, with the Fold 8 starting near $1,900 and the Ultra around $2,100. Worth watching the value, not just the hype. 📱
posts.problem: Foldables have gotten thinner and more refined every generation, but that progress has consistently come with rising prices, making 'better' and 'more expensive' arrive together every single cycle.
posts.breakthrough: Samsung Galaxy Unpacked is officially set for July 22 with a foldable-heavy lineup — the Galaxy Z Fold 8, a larger Z Fold 8 Ultra, a lighter Z Flip 8 (around 180g), the Watch 9 series, and possibly a first look at Galaxy Glasses — with the Fold 8 starting near $1,900 and the Ultra around $2,100.
posts.catch: The creases keep shrinking, but so does the value proposition at these prices — a thinner hinge doesn't automatically justify a bigger bill.
posts.forYou: Before you get caught up in the reveal, decide your actual price ceiling now — at $1,900 to $2,100, this is a purchase worth budgeting for deliberately, not one to impulse-preorder on hype.

posts.hook: GPT-5.6 is now ChatGPT's default model
posts.body: GPT-5.6 is now the default model inside ChatGPT. It's stronger at coding, reasoning and long context (a full 1M-token window), and the balanced 'Terra' tier reportedly matches the older GPT-5.5 at roughly half the cost. The best part: there's no setting to flip — the upgrade just landed for everyone. 🤖
posts.problem: Model upgrades usually require users to manually opt in or discover a new setting, meaning a lot of people keep using an older, weaker model without realizing a better one is available.
posts.breakthrough: GPT-5.6 is now the default model inside ChatGPT for everyone, with no setting to flip — stronger at coding, reasoning, and long context with a full 1M-token window, and the balanced 'Terra' tier reportedly matches the older GPT-5.5 at roughly half the cost.
posts.catch: Because it rolled out silently, some users may not notice their experience changed at all, even though the underlying model and its cost profile did.
posts.forYou: If you use ChatGPT regularly, you're already on GPT-5.6 whether you noticed or not — worth re-testing tasks you'd previously written off as too weak for the model, since the upgrade is free and automatic.

posts.hook: The Gmail '+tag' trick that catches who sold your email
posts.body: One Gmail trick most people miss: add a '+tag' before the @, like yourname+shopping@gmail.com. Mail still reaches your normal inbox, but now you can auto-filter it by tag and, crucially, spot exactly who leaked or sold your address when spam starts arriving at a tagged version. Zero setup, works today. 💡
posts.problem: When spam starts arriving at your email address, it's almost impossible to know which company or website actually sold or leaked it — every service you've ever signed up for is a suspect.
posts.breakthrough: Adding a '+tag' before the @ in your Gmail address, like yourname+shopping@gmail.com, still delivers to your normal inbox, but lets you filter by tag and identify exactly which signup leaked your address once spam starts arriving at that specific tagged version.
posts.catch: Some sign-up forms reject the '+' character as invalid, so it doesn't work universally — and once you know who leaked it, you still can't undo the leak itself.
posts.forYou: Start tagging new sign-ups today, especially ones you're wary of — it costs zero setup and turns 'who sold my email' from a mystery into a quick filter search.

posts.hook: ChatGPT vs Gemini vs Claude — the honest mid-2026 split
posts.body: 'Which AI is best?' is the wrong question in mid-2026. The honest split: Claude leads for coding and agents (Fable 5 tops SWE-Bench Pro), ChatGPT is the everyday and voice pick (GPT-5.6 is now default), and Gemini wins if you live in Google apps and search. All three now handle 1M-token context for long documents, and Gemini 3.5 Pro is still delayed. There's no universal winner — keep two open and match the tool to the task. 🤖
posts.problem: The 'which AI is best' question keeps getting asked as a single-answer competition, when the honest answer depends entirely on whether you're coding, chatting, or living inside Google's ecosystem.
posts.breakthrough: The honest mid-2026 split: Claude leads for coding and agents (Fable 5 tops SWE-Bench Pro), ChatGPT is the everyday and voice pick with GPT-5.6 now default, and Gemini wins if you live in Google apps and search — with all three now handling 1M-token context for long documents, while Gemini 3.5 Pro remains delayed.
posts.catch: Gemini's positioning here is weaker than it might otherwise be specifically because 3.5 Pro hasn't shipped — its ranking could shift the moment that changes.
posts.forYou: Keep two of these open rather than committing to one: Claude for coding and agents, ChatGPT for daily use and voice, Gemini if your work already lives in Google apps.

posts.hook: More megapixels doesn't mean a better camera
posts.body: Time to bust a spec-sheet myth: more megapixels does not mean better photos. What actually decides image quality is sensor size, pixel-binning (combining pixels for cleaner low-light shots), and image processing. That's why a 48MP iPhone or Pixel can out-shoot a 200MP spec sheet. If you're phone-shopping this launch season, stop counting megapixels and look at the sensor and software instead. 📸
posts.problem: Phone marketing keeps leaning on megapixel counts every launch season, and it keeps working on buyers even though the number has stopped correlating with actual photo quality.
posts.breakthrough: What actually decides image quality is sensor size, pixel-binning (combining pixels for cleaner low-light shots), and image processing — which is why a 48MP iPhone or Pixel can consistently out-shoot a phone with a 200MP spec sheet.
posts.catch: This doesn't mean megapixels are meaningless — they matter for cropping and certain zoom modes — just that they're not the number that determines whether your everyday photos actually look good.
posts.forYou: If you're phone-shopping this launch season, stop comparing megapixel counts and look for real sensor size and processing details instead — that's what will actually show up in your photos.
